structuredFacts.title: DNA
structuredFacts.subtitle: Molecule that carries genetic information
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Deoxyribonucleic acid (; DNA) is a polymer composed of two polynucleotide chains that coil around each other to form a double helix. The polymer carries genetic instructions for the development, functioning, growth and reproduction of all known organisms and many viruses. DNA and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are nucleic acids. Alongside proteins, lipids and complex carbohydrates (polysaccharides), nucleic acids are one of the four major types of macromolecules that are essential for all known forms of life.
The two DNA strands are known as polynucleotides as they are composed of simpler monomeric units called nucleotides. Each nucleotide is composed of one of four nitrogen-containing nucleobases (cytosine [C], guanine [G], adenine [A] or thymine [T]), a sugar called deoxyribose, and a phosphate group. The nucleotides are joined to one another in a chain by covalent bonds (known as the phosphodiester linkage) between the sugar of one nucleotide and the phosphate of the next, resulting in an alternating sugar-phosphate backbone. The nitrogenous bases of the two separate polynucleotide strands are bound together, according to base pairing rules (A with T and C with G), with hydrogen bonds to make double-stranded DNA. The complementary nitrogenous bases are divided into two groups, the single-ringed pyrimidines and the double-ringed purines. In DNA, the pyrimidines are thymine and cytosine; the purines are adenine and guanine.
Both strands of double-stranded DNA store the same biological information. This information is replicated when the two strands separate. The two strands of DNA run in opposite directions to each other and are thus antiparallel. Attached to each sugar is one of four types of nucleobases (or bases). It is the sequence of these four nucleobases along the backbone that encodes genetic information. RNA strands are created using DNA strands as a template in a process called transcription, where DNA bases are exchanged for their corresponding bases except in the case of thymine (T), for which RNA substitutes uracil (U). Under the genetic code, these RNA strands specify the sequence of amino acids within proteins in a process called translation.
Within eukaryotic cells, DNA is organized into long structures called chromosomes. Before typical cell division, these chromosomes are duplicated in the process of DNA replication, providing a complete set of chromosomes for each daughter cell. Eukaryotic organisms (animals, plants, fungi and protists) store most of their DNA inside the cell nucleus as nuclear DNA, and some in the mitochondria as mitochondrial DNA or in chloroplasts as chloroplast DNA. In contrast, prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea) store their DNA only in the cytoplasm, in circular chromosomes. Within eukaryotic chromosomes, chromatin proteins, such as histones, compact and organize DNA. These compacting structures guide the interactions between DNA and other proteins, helping control which parts of the DNA are transcribed.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: As a double-stranded polymer, DNA carries the fundamental genetic instructions for all living organisms. While eukaryotic organisms organize and store this genetic material within membrane-bound organelles—specifically the nucleus, mitochondria, or chloroplasts—prokaryotes lack these internal membranes and keep their DNA directly within the cellular cytoplasm. Scientists first identified a non-canonical DNA base, 5-methylcytosine, in the genome of Mycobacterium tuberculosis in 1925. Modern research now investigates other non-canonical bases, including 5-hydroxymethylcytosine, 5-formylcytosine, and 5-carboxylcytosine. Notably, bacterial viruses incorporate these modified bases into their own genomes to evade the restriction enzymes of host bacteria.

cards.title: Architects of the Genome
cards.summary: As a double-stranded polymer, DNA carries the fundamental genetic instructions for all living organisms. To protect massive volumes of vital biological data, this molecular blueprint requires systematic organization. Eukaryotes, for instance, store their DNA within specialized, membrane-bound organelles like the nucleus. Consequently, these complex organisms evolved highly divergent spatial arrangements to isolate their genomic material. Prokaryotes, by contrast, lack membrane-bound nuclei entirely. Ultimately, cells organize their genetic material based on their evolutionary complexity.
cards.readerQuestion: How do different organisms organize and store their genetic instructions within their cells?
cards.keyTakeaway: Cellular complexity dictates how organisms organize and store genetic instructions.

cards.title: The Hidden Genetic Alphabet
cards.summary: In 1925, scientists discovered the first non-canonical DNA base, 5-methylcytosine, within the genome of Mycobacterium tuberculosis . This breakthrough occurred because researchers initiated a rigorous chemical investigation into the composition of bacterial genomes . Modified bases like 5-methylcytosine occur naturally within the genetic structure of DNA . This discovery proved that genomes contain modified bases beyond the standard alphabet, laying the groundwork for modern epigenetics . Standard DNA contains the familiar nitrogen-containing nucleobases of Cytosine, Guanine, Adenine, and Thymine . Ultimately, genetic complexity extends far beyond these four standard bases .
cards.readerQuestion: When did scientists first discover that DNA contains more than just the standard genetic bases?
cards.keyTakeaway: DNA complexity extends beyond the four standard genetic bases.

cards.title: The Architecture of Life
cards.summary: Each nucleotide in DNA contains a 2-deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases. Driven by their chemical properties and specific bonding requirements, these nucleotides spontaneously assemble. Covalent 3'-carbon to 5'-carbon phosphodiester bonds link the sugar-phosphate backbone in opposite, antiparallel directions. This precise structural orientation provides a highly stable, directional, and self-complementary medium for storing genetic information. To stabilize the resulting double helix, hydrogen bonds form between complementary bases alongside hydrophobic base-stacking interactions. Ultimately, these chemical bonds shape the stable, antiparallel genetic ladder.
cards.readerQuestion: How do nucleotides and chemical bonds construct the physical architecture of the double helix?
cards.keyTakeaway: Chemical bonds shape the stable, antiparallel genetic ladder.

provenance.sentence: Each nucleotide in DNA contains a 2-deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases.
provenance.fact: DNA consists of nucleotides made of a 2-deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C), or guanine (G).

provenance.sentence: Driven by their chemical properties and specific bonding requirements, these nucleotides spontaneously assemble.
provenance.fact: The chemical properties of nucleotides and their specific bonding requirements drive the spontaneous assembly of the double helix structure.

provenance.fact: The sugar-phosphate backbone is highly directional, running in opposite, antiparallel directions for each of the two strands via alternating phosphate and sugar groups joined by covalent 3'-carbon to 5'-carbon phosphodiester bonds.

provenance.sentence: This precise structural orientation provides a highly stable, directional, and self-complementary medium for storing genetic information.
provenance.fact: The resulting antiparallel double helix provides a highly stable, directional, and self-complementary medium for storing genetic information.

provenance.sentence: To stabilize the resulting double helix, hydrogen bonds form between complementary bases alongside hydrophobic base-stacking interactions.
provenance.fact: The double helix structure is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs (A-T and C-G) and hydrophobic base-stacking interactions among the aromatic nucleobases.

provenance.sentence: Ultimately, these chemical bonds shape the stable, antiparallel genetic ladder.
provenance.fact: Chemical bonds shape the stable, antiparallel genetic ladder.

cards.title: The Geometry of Life
cards.summary: A single helical turn of standard B-DNA spans exactly 34 ångströms in length. This precise spatial geometry is dictated by the physical constraints of nucleotide base-pairing and sugar-phosphate backbones. Structurally, nitrogenous bases are classified into double-ringed purines and single-ringed pyrimidines. Such exact dimensions allow DNA to pack tightly within cells while remaining accessible for replication. Most DNA has a buoyant density of 1.7 grams per cubic centimeter. Ultimately, standard B-DNA possesses precise, dense, and highly ordered helical dimensions.
cards.readerQuestion: What are the exact physical measurements and structural classifications of standard DNA?
cards.keyTakeaway: Standard B-DNA maintains precise, dense, and highly ordered dimensions.

provenance.fact: These exact dimensions allow DNA to pack tightly within cells while remaining accessible for replication and transcription machinery.

cards.title: The Genomic Arms Race
cards.summary: Bacterial viruses incorporate modified, non-canonical bases into their genomes to evade host restriction enzymes . Organisms evolved these chemical modifications to protect genomes or control gene expression without altering sequences . DNA modifications, like cytosine and adenine methylation, drive eukaryotic epigenetic gene regulation in plants and animals . These adaptations established complex epigenetic systems and an ongoing evolutionary arms race . The modified base 5-methylcytosine was first identified in Mycobacterium tuberculosis in 1925 . Ultimately, genomic modifications drive vital cellular defense and epigenetic regulation .
cards.readerQuestion: How do organisms use genomic modifications to regulate genes and defend against viral invaders?
cards.keyTakeaway: Genomic modifications drive vital cellular defense and epigenetic regulation.

provenance.fact: These molecular adaptations established a complex biological system of epigenetic gene regulation in eukaryotes and an ongoing evolutionary arms race between bacterial restriction enzymes and viral genomic modifications.
